items.title: Writing About Literature: Writing About Love
items.source_text: The course examines intimate bonds and heartbreak across multiple genres. As the syllabus states, students will "Read for love" and analyze how consciousness is shaped by relationships, particularly exploring "the lover's (un)becoming" through literature and other media. Students develop critical thinking by analyzing representations of "racial love, gendered love, and queer love" across periods and cultures.

items.title: Foundations of Western Culture: The Making of the Modern World
items.source_text: This MIT literature course surveys texts from the 17th to early 20th centuries, examining "the difference between the ancient and the modern world" through philosophical and literary works. The curriculum emphasizes how individualism emerged as humanity reconsidered its place in the universe. All work emphasizes "close analysis" of specific passages, balancing textual detail with broader interpretation.

items.title: How to Stage a Revolution
items.source_text: The course explores fundamental questions about revolutions, including how people overthrow rulers, establish new governments, and whether radical change requires violence. Students examine three major late 18th/early 19th-century examples: American, French, and Haitian Revolutions, plus contemporary Egypt (2011 onward). The course uses diverse materials including "writings of revolutionaries, declarations and constitutions, music, films, art, memoirs, and newspapers."

items.title: American History Since 1865
items.source_text: The course provides "an introduction to the social, political, economic, and cultural history of the United States – from the end of the American Civil War to the present." Students examine both secondary historical accounts and primary source documents to understand major transformations shaping modern America. Key areas of focus include: Conflict and settlement dynamics in the frontier West; Immigration patterns and industrial development; U.S. emergence as a global power; Cold War: domestic and international dimensions; Civil Rights activism and social movements; The digital age transition.

items.title: Good Food: Ethics and Politics of Food
items.source_text: The course examines how food choices reflect aesthetic, moral, cultural, and religious values. It addresses key ethical questions including obligations to non-human animals, connections between personal eating choices and global justice, consumer information responsibilities, and individual duties regarding climate change. Students develop capacity to identify moral and political issues in food debates, evaluate relevant evidence, apply normative ethical frameworks, refine personal dietary perspectives, and communicate effectively about controversial topics.

items.title: Musical Improvisation
items.source_text: The course exposes students to improvisation's multifaceted nature across diverse traditions. As stated, it aims to provide "an overview of and an immersion in a variety of approaches to musical improvisation." Students examine "concepts and techniques of improvisation in solo and ensemble contexts." The course investigates relationships among improvisation, composition, and performance, exploring both traditional and experimental methodologies.
